---
title: "Course Specification: Analysis, Management, Data Mining, and Visualisation of Big Data"
subtitle: "Master's Degree Program - Core Module"
author:
  - name: Orélie Desfriches Doria
    affiliations:
      - name: Laboratoire paragraphe
      - name: Département Information-Documentation
      - name: Université Paris 8 Vincennes-Saint-Denis
  - name: Samuel Szoniecky
    affiliations:
      - name: Laboratoire paragraphe
      - name: Département Information-Documentation
      - name: Université Paris 8 Vincennes-Saint-Denis
date: "2026"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    theme: cosmo
---

# 1. Module Overview

This module provides an intensive introduction to the lifecycle of Big Data. Students will transition from handling static, small-scale datasets to managing the velocity, volume, and variety of modern data environments. The course balances technical rigors (data engineering) with creative storytelling (data visualization).

# 2. Learning Outcomes

By the end of this course, students will be able to: 1. **Architect** data pipelines for acquisition, cleaning, and transformation. 2. **Evaluate** storage solutions for structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data (SQL vs. NoSQL). 3. **Apply** data mining techniques (Classification, Clustering, Regression) to extract patterns. 4. **Synthesize** complex findings into interactive dashboards and visual stories. 5. **Critique** data practices through the lens of methodological rigor and ethical responsibility.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 3. The 12-Week Roadmap

| Week | Phase | Topic | Key Tools (2026 Stack) |
|:-----------------|:-----------------|:-----------------|:-----------------|
| **W1** | **Foundations** | The 5 Vs of Big Data: Challenges & Opportunities | Apache Spark, Hadoop MapReduce |
| **W2** | **Foundations** | Data Acquisition & ETL Workflows (Cleaning & Prep) | Python (Pandas), dbt |
| **W3** | **Management** | Storage: SQL vs. NoSQL & Data Lakes | Snowflake, MongoDB, S3 |
| **W4** | **Management** | Processing Large-Scale Data Environments | Databricks, PySpark |
| **W5** | **Analysis** | Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) & Descriptive Stats | Jupyter Labs, Seaborn |
| **W6** | **Mining** | Supervised Learning: Classification & Regression | Scikit-Learn |
| **W7** | **Mining** | Unsupervised Learning: Clustering & Association | Scikit-Learn |
| **W8** | **Mining** | Interpreting Patterns, Trends & Relationships | SHAP, LIME (Model XAI) |
| **W9** | **Visualisation** | Principles of Visual Perception & Design | Tableau / Power BI |
| **W10** | **Visualisation** | Interactive Dashboards & Real-time Monitoring | Streamlit / Plotly Dash |
| **W11** | **Strategy** | Visual Storytelling & Decision Support | Canva, Quarto (Interactive Reports) |
| **W12** | **Capstone** | Final Project Defense: Real-world Big Data Case | Full Stack |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 4. Assessment Methodology

### 4.1 Continuous Assessment (40%)

-   **Bi-weekly Coding Labs (20%):** Hands-on data cleaning and mining exercises.
-   **Mid-term Data Audit (20%):** Critiquing a provided "dirty" dataset for ethical and structural flaws.

### 4.2 Final Capstone Project (60%)

Students must select a realistic large-scale dataset (e.g., Global Weather, Logistics, or Financial Transactions) and perform a full "Raw-to-Insight" pipeline.

-   **Technical Pipeline (20%):** The ETL and Processing logic.
-   **Mining Model (20%):** The choice and accuracy of the mining technique.
-   **Visual Story (20%):** An interactive dashboard presenting the decision-support findings.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 5. Ethical & Responsible Use

In 2026, Big Data is inseparable from **AI Ethics**. Throughout the module, we will address:

-   **Bias Mitigation:** Ensuring datasets do not reinforce historical inequities.

-   **Privacy-Preserving Analytics:** Introduction to Differential Privacy.

-   **Rigorous Validation:** Avoiding "data dredging" (p-hacking).

::: callout-note
### Prerequisites

Basic proficiency in Python and SQL is expected. Students unfamiliar with these should complete the "Data Prep Bootcamp" in the Welcome Kit.
:::
